perturbation.original_text: Contractor shall provide copies of such agreements to the State. The names of such personnel are attached hereto and made a part hereof as Attach ment I-2. Contractor shall update Attachment I-2 by adding additional names (whether Contractor’s Personnel or a subcontractor’s personnel) as needed, from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor may provide copies of such agreements to the State at their discretion. The names of such personnel may be attached hereto as Attachment I-2.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to provide copies of the agreements and update the list of personnel weakens the State's ability to enforce compliance with data protection requirements, which is a privacy control.
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland law requires that agencies implement reasonable security measures to protect personal information. By making the provision of confidentiality agreements and personnel lists discretionary, the contract weakens the enforcement and oversight of these security measures, potentially leading to a violation of the requirement to implement reasonable security measures.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Section 10-624 - Personal records. :: 2010 Maryland Code :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Maryland Code 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code 2010 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2010 Maryland Code STATE GOVERNMENT TITLE 10 - GOVERNMENTAL PROCEDURES Subtitle 6 - Records Section 10-624 - Personal records. § 10-624. Personal records. (a) "Personal record" defined.- In this section, "personal record" means a public record that names or, with reasonable certainty, otherwise identifies an individual by an identifying factor such as: (1) an address; (2) a description; (3) a finger or voice print; (4) a number; or (5) a picture. (b) Requirement of need.- (1) Personal records may not be created unless the need for the information has been clearly established by the unit collecting the records. (2) Personal information collected for personal records: (i) shall be appropriate and relevant to the purposes for which it is collected; (ii) shall be accurate and current to the greatest extent practicable; and (iii) may not be obtained by fraudulent means. (c) Collection by official custodian from person in interest.- (1) This subsection only applies to units of State government. (2) Except as otherwise provided by law, an official custodian who keeps personal records shall, to the greatest extent practicable, collect personal information from the person in interest. (3) An official custodian who requests personal information for personal records shall provide the following information to each person in interest from whom personal information is collected: (i) the purpose for which the personal information is collected; (ii) any specific consequences to the person for refusal to provide the personal information; (iii) the person's right to inspect, amend, or correct personal records, if any; (iv) whether the personal information is generally available for public inspection; and (v) whether the pers
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the contractor to provide copies of agreements and update personnel lists, while the changed text makes these actions discretionary. The Law Explanation highlights that Maryland law requires agencies to implement reasonable security measures to protect personal information. The omission of the mandatory provision weakens the enforcement and oversight of these security measures, potentially leading to a violation of implementing reasonable security measures. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Section 10-624, discusses personal records and the need for appropriate collection, relevance, and accuracy. By removing the mandatory provision for providing agreements and personnel lists, the changed text reduces the State's ability to ensure that the collection and maintenance of personal records are appropriate and relevant.

perturbation.original_text: The Contractor shall promptly advise the State in writing if it learns of any unauthorized use, misappropriation, or disclosure of the Confidential Information by any of the Contractor’s Personnel or the Contractor’s former Personnel. Contractor shall, at its own e xpense, cooperate with the State in seeking injunctive or other equitable relief against any such person(s).
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor may advise the State if it learns of any unauthorized use, misappropriation, or disclosure of the Confidential Information. Contractor may cooperate with the State in seeking injunctive or other equitable relief.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'may' removes the mandatory obligation for the contractor to report breaches and cooperate with the State, which weakens data breach notification requirements and the State's ability to respond effectively. Removing 'at its own expense' absolves the contractor of the financial burden of cooperation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland law requires notification of a breach of the security of a system. By changing the language to permissive rather than mandatory, the contract weakens the notification requirement and hinders the State's ability to comply with its legal obligations under Maryland's breach notification law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Section 10-623 - Judicial review. :: 2010 Maryland Code :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Maryland Code 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code 2010 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2010 Maryland Code STATE GOVERNMENT TITLE 10 - GOVERNMENTAL PROCEDURES Subtitle 6 - Records Section 10-623 - Judicial review. § 10-623. Judicial review. (a) Petition authorized.- Whenever a person or governmental unit is denied inspection of a public record, the person or governmental unit may file a complaint with the circuit court for the county where: (1) the complainant resides or has a principal place of business; or (2) the public record is located. (b) Defendant.- (1) Unless, for good cause shown, the court otherwise directs and notwithstanding any other provision of law, the defendant shall serve an answer or otherwise plead to the complaint within 30 days after service of the complaint. (2) The defendant: (i) has the burden of sustaining a decision to deny inspection of a public record; and (ii) in support of the decision, may submit a memorandum to the court. (c) Court.- (1) Except for cases that the court considers of greater importance, a proceeding under this section, including an appeal, shall: (i) take precedence on the docket; (ii) be heard at the earliest practicable date; and (iii) be expedited in every way. (2) The court may examine the public record in camera to determine whether any part of it may be withheld under this Part III of this subtitle. (3) The court may: (i) enjoin the State, a political subdivision, or a unit, official, or employee of the State or of a political subdivision from withholding the public record; (ii) pass an order for the production of the public record that was withheld from the complainant; and (iii) for noncompliance with the order, punish the responsible employee for contempt. perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall' to 'may' removes the mandatory obligation for the contractor to report data breaches and cooperate with the State. This directly contradicts the Maryland law requiring notification of a security breach, as explained in the Law Explanation. The omission of 'at its own expense' further weakens the obligation. Although the scraped snippets do not explicitly state this obligation, the Law Explanation fills in that gap.
